text: The surgeon performs splenic flexure mobilization using a medial to lateral dissection technique, entering below the inferior mesenteric vein while preserving retroperitoneal structures.

After completing the dissection, the surgeon places a swab to protect the retroperitoneal structures. The inferior mesenteric vein is then ligated and divided, marking a critical step in the procedure.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac from above the pancreas, confirming entry by visualizing the posterior wall of the stomach. The focus shifts to gaining entry into the lesser sac from above the transverse colon.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the transverse mesocolon, avoiding disruption, while progressing towards the spleen. The presence of increased adipose tissue and fused planes makes this stage challenging.
The surgeon observes patchy ischemia in the transverse colon while retracting it cranially, revealing a large defect in the transverse mesocolon with disrupted blood supply to the transverse colon.
The surgeon demonstrates the mobilization of the transverse colon up to the hepatic flexure from an overhead view, showing the progression of the dissection.
The surgeon assesses the transverse colon specimen, noting that it must be resected proximally due to ischemia, confirming that the colon cannot reach the pelvis.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection to take the inferior mesenteric artery, completing the dissection to mobilize the entire left colon.

A sizer is inserted into the rectum to ensure that a surgical stapler can pass through. The rectum is prepared for transection, and the specimen is extracted following the completion of right colon mobilization.
The surgeon extracts the specimen and performs excision. The colonic conduit is then passed back into the abdomen through a glove port, with an anvil positioned at the colonic end.
The surgeon focuses on the terminal ileum, identifying an avascular plane near the ileocolic pedicle. A space is created transmesenterically within this avascular plane.

The surgeon carefully avoids disrupting significant vasculature, including the ileocolic pedicle, while extending the peritoneal component of the mesentery to create a void for the conduit to pass through.

The surgeon ensures the safe passage of the conduit to the opposite peritoneal side of the mesentery, maintaining a bloodless field. The terminal ileum is fully mobilized from lateral attachments before proceeding.
The surgeon enlarges the defect to facilitate the passage of the conduit. The terminal ileum is manipulated to allow the conduit and anvil to pass through the created defect.

The conduit is visualized passing safely down to the pelvis, facilitating a tension-free anastomosis. A circular stapler gun is inserted through the rectum, and a spike is introduced to perform the anastomosis, ensuring the conduit is correctly oriented to prevent twisting. An air leak test is conducted to confirm the integrity of the anastomosis.
